title: Space Telescope Unveils Ancient Galaxy Cluster
story: Astronomers were thrilled on July 11, 2022, when NASA and President Biden revealed an incredible image from the James Webb Space Telescope. The picture showed SMACS 0723, a massive cluster of galaxies. These cosmic wonders appeared as they looked about 13 billion years ago, offering a glimpse into the early universe. The light from this cluster traveled an astonishing 4 billion light-years to reach the telescope's powerful eye.
activity_question: What did the James Webb Space Telescope image reveal?
activity_choices: A new planet in our solar system
follow_up_question: How many billion years old did the galaxies in the image appear to be?

title: Space Robot's Daring Asteroid Landing
story: A brave little spacecraft named Hayabusa2 pulled off an amazing feat in 2019. On July 11, it touched down on a faraway asteroid called Ryugu for the second time. This wasn't just any landing - Hayabusa2 was there to scoop up precious samples from the asteroid's surface. Imagine a robot exploring a giant space rock nearly 300 million kilometers from Earth!
activity_question: What was the name of the spacecraft that landed on the asteroid?
follow_up_question: Why do you think scientists want to study samples from asteroids?

title: Dogs' Super Sniffers Help Them 'See'
story: Sniff, sniff! Dogs' amazing noses just got even cooler. In 2022, scientists made a mind-blowing discovery about our furry friends. They found that dogs' brains have a special connection between their smell center and vision area. This means pups might actually 'see' with their noses! It's the first time this link has been found in any animal. No wonder dogs are such great sniffers and helpers!
activity_question: What special connection did scientists discover in dogs' brains?
activity_choices: Between their smell center and vision area
activity_answer: Between their smell center and vision area
follow_up_question: How might this discovery help explain why dogs are such good helpers?

title: Neptune's Epic Journey Around the Sun
story: Imagine a planet so far away it takes 165 years to circle the sun! On July 11, 2011, Neptune finished its first full orbit since humans spotted it in 1846. This giant blue world, cold and stormy, finally completed its cosmic lap. Scientists celebrated this milestone, marking a new chapter in our understanding of the solar system's outer reaches.
activity_question: How long did it take Neptune to complete one orbit around the sun?
bonus_fact: Neptune is so cold that its winds are the fastest in the solar system, reaching speeds of about 1,200 mph!

title: Pluto's Hidden Moon Revealed
story: Stargazers got a thrilling surprise in 2012 when they spotted a tiny new moon orbiting Pluto! On July 11, astronomers using the Hubble Space Telescope discovered S/2012 P 1, Pluto's fifth known moon. This cosmic pebble is so small and dim that it had escaped notice for years. Its discovery reminds us that even in our own solar system, there are still secrets waiting to be uncovered.
activity_question: What instrument was used to discover Pluto's fifth moon?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it took so long to discover this moon of Pluto?

title: Ancient Tools Rewrite Human History
story: Archaeologists made a groundbreaking discovery in 2018! In Lantian County, western China, they unearthed the oldest stone tools ever found outside Africa. These ancient artifacts, crafted by early human ancestors called hominins, are an incredible 2.12 million years old. The find on July 11 pushed back the timeline of human migration from Africa, showing our ancestors ventured out much earlier than previously thought.
activity_question: Where were the oldest stone tools outside Africa discovered?
follow_up_question: What do you think these ancient tools were used for?

title: Tijuana's Colossal Cocktail Conquers Record Books
story: Salty rims and lime slices galore! Tijuana, Mexico, shook up the record books on July 11, 2025, with a margarita so massive it could quench the thirst of an entire city. Mixologists and volunteers poured their hearts out, creating a whopping 34,419-liter cocktail. That's about as much liquid as several backyard swimming pools combined! The giant drink clinched a new Guinness World Record for the largest margarita ever made.

title: The Man Who Made Light Work
story: Zap! Theodore Harold Maiman was born on July 11, 1927, in Los Angeles. This bright spark would grow up to invent something that changed the world: the laser! Maiman's creation focused light into a powerful beam. Today, lasers help us do everything from scanning groceries to performing delicate surgeries. Maiman's invention truly made light work for us all.
activity_question: What did Theodore Harold Maiman invent?
bonus_fact: The word 'laser' is actually an acronym that stands for 'Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation'.

title: A Cosmic Tune Takes Flight
story: Rocket engines roared as David Bowie launched his musical career into the stratosphere. On July 11, 1969, the British singer-songwriter released Space Oddity, a haunting tune about an astronaut's adventure. The song's timing was stellar, hitting airwaves just days before Apollo 11's historic moon landing. Bowie's cosmic melody captured the world's excitement about space exploration, blending fact and fantasy in a way that would define his artistic journey.

title: Charlotte Cooper: Tennis Trailblazer
story: Rackets clashed and the crowd cheered as Charlotte Cooper made history. On July 11, 1900, she became the first female Olympic tennis champion, defeating Hélène Prévost in an exciting match. But Cooper's victory was even bigger than that. She also became the very first individual female Olympic champion in any sport, paving the way for countless women athletes to follow in her footsteps.
activity_question: What did Charlotte Cooper become the first to achieve?

The first male Olympic tennis champion
The first female Olympic swimming champion
The first female Olympic gymnastics champion
activity_answer: The first female Olympic tennis champion

story: Sparks flew in the world of invention! On July 11, 1892, the US Patent Office made a shocking announcement. title: Skylab's Fiery Farewell
story: Skylab, America's first space station, made a spectacular exit in 1979. On July 11, the massive structure plunged through Earth's atmosphere, breaking apart in a dazzling light show over Australia. Pieces of the 77-ton station scattered across the Indian Ocean and Western Australia. Luckily, no one was hurt by the falling debris. Skylab's dramatic return marked the end of its groundbreaking mission in space exploration.
activity_question: Where did Skylab break apart?
bonus_fact: Some Australians collected Skylab pieces as souvenirs!

title: Pluto's Cosmic Dance
story: Pluto played a celestial game of leapfrog in 1735! Mathematical calculations show that on July 11 of that year, this icy world shifted from being the ninth to the eighth most distant 'planet' from the Sun. It was the last time Pluto would make this cosmic move before 1979. This dance through space reminds us how dynamic our solar system can be!
activity_question: What did Pluto change from being on July 11, 1735?
activity_choices: The ninth to the eighth most distant 'planet' from the Sun
The smallest to the largest dwarf planet
A rocky planet to an ice giant
A moon of Neptune to its own planet
activity_answer: The ninth to the eighth most distant 'planet' from the Sun

title: Telstar I: TV's Giant Leap Across Oceans
story: Zap! On July 11, 1962, TV signals bounced off a satellite for the first time, leaping across the Atlantic Ocean. Telstar I, a shiny metal ball covered in antennas, made history. It beamed images between America and Europe, shrinking our world. Suddenly, people could watch events happening on another continent, live! This space-age breakthrough changed how we share information forever.
activity_question: What did Telstar I do for the first time?
activity_choices: Beamed images between America and Europe
activity_answer: Beamed images between America and Europe
bonus_fact: Telstar I was about the size of a beach ball and weighed only 170 pounds!

title: Hockey Legend Jean-Guy Talbot's Icy Journey
story: Skates sliced the ice as Jean-Guy Talbot glided into hockey history. Born in 1932 on July 11 in Quebec, this Canadian defenceman became a true ice warrior. Talbot's stick skills led him to hoist the Stanley Cup seven times with the Montreal Canadiens. He also earned six NHL All-Star nods before trading his player jersey for a coach's whistle with the St. Louis Blues and New York Rangers.
